Foundations — Typography

The codified Precision Blue type scale, locked 2026-07-21 alongside the color model (full trail: context/design-language.html). Hierarchy is carried by the size and weight of numerals — never by color — so this page stays deliberately monochrome apart from the section tags it borrows from foundations/colors.html. This page is generated to match the charter's Typography section exactly; if a screen disagrees with this page, the screen is wrong.

1. The scale — live, largest first · locked

Exactly seven roles exist. Each row below is rendered at its real size, weight, and tracking — not described, shown.

SpecimenSpecRole
315 lb 34px · 800 · tabular-nums · −0.5px Hero numeral — the biggest number on a screen. Today's working weight, an estimated 1RM headline. Wins hierarchy by size and weight alone.
Workout 20px · 800 · −0.3px Screen title — the page-level heading. Tight negative tracking.
Bench Press 14px · 700 Card name — the exercise or entity named at the top of a card.
185 lb × 5 13px · 600 · tabular-nums Value — a logged or prescribed set's weight × reps.
4 × 6 @ 82.5 kg · RPE 8 13px · 400 · var(--text-2) Body — prescriptions, captions, secondary sentences.
Estimated 1RM 9.5px · 700 · uppercase · +1.1px Eyebrow — the signature label form. Small and tracked wide.
2 11px · 700 · tabular-nums · var(--text-muted) Index / muted — set numbers, disabled state.

2. Laws in type · locked

Three rules govern every numeral and label in the system — restated from the charter's mindset laws with nothing added.

  • The biggest number wins. Hierarchy is carried by the size and weight of numerals — tabular, tight — never by color. The most important number on a screen should be findable with peripheral vision; a hero numeral is never recolored to compete, it simply outsizes and outweighs everything around it.
  • Numerals are always tabular. Every numeral role above — hero, value, index — sets font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums. Digits never reflow a row as they change mid-set.
  • Titles track tight, eyebrows track wide — the signature tension. Screen titles and hero numerals pull their tracking in (−0.3px / −0.5px); eyebrows push theirs out (+1.1px). The two directions never meet in the middle — that opposition is the typographic signature of the whole system.

If a screen needs a colored badge to show what matters most, the type hierarchy has failed — restructure with size and weight instead, per the charter's structure law. A third hue is never the fix for a typography problem.

3. Wordmark · locked

The one exception to the system stack. Orbitron 800 is reserved exclusively for the ΛPEX wordmark — app icon, splash screen, settings header — and appears nowhere else in the type system; none of the seven roles in §1 ever borrow it. It is not loaded on this page (this document is self-contained, zero external requests); the specimen below approximates Orbitron's geometric, wide-set character using the system stack plus letter-spacing only.

ΛPEX

System-stack approximation only — 800 weight, wide positive tracking, no italic or condensed substitute. The production face is Orbitron 800, reserved exclusively for this wordmark; it is never substituted into a screen title, a card name, or any other role on this page.

4. Dynamic Type · locked

Every size in §1 is a reference value at the default Dynamic Type scale (the system's standard text size, mid-way through the accessibility range) — not a fixed pixel constant. In SwiftUI, each role maps to a text style at build time (for example, the hero numeral is a custom 34pt / .heavy style registered against a scaling curve, not a hand-set literal) so it grows and shrinks with the user's chosen text size. Numerals stay tabular at every scale step; tracking scales proportionally with size, it does not stay fixed in points.

This page cannot demonstrate scaling — a static document has no Dynamic Type setting to honor. Treat every number in §1 as "at default," and verify scaled behavior in the SwiftUI Foundation layer, not here.
